EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high wind gust of 59 MPH occurred at the Burlington airport.  Meanwhile to the northwest a line of thunderstorms caused a power outage in the Yuma area.  As the thunderstorms continued southeast and weakened, they produced wind gusts near Bethune strong enough to cause near zero visibility in blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility fell to near zero in the blowing dust from thunderstorm winds near the intersection of CR 43 and I-70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the day unseasonably strong winds occurred in East Central Colorado.  The peak wind gust reported was 62 MPH at the Burlington airport and near First View.  These high winds also caused damage near Eckley to an outbuilding, vehicle, trailer, and a few small trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported 4 E of Firstview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During June the drought status for East Central Colorado worsened.  The D2 and D3 values crept northward, with the D2 drought extending as far north as western Yuma County.  The D3 drought extended into southwest Kit Carson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Almost all of the county was encompassed by D2 or D3 drought, with the D3 drought being confined to the southwest part of the county.  Precipitation amounts ranged from 0.52 over the west part of the county to 2.67 over the central part of the county.  These amounts were 2.27 to 0.17 below normal respectively for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During June the drought status for East Central Colorado worsened. The D2 values crept northward, with the D2 drought extending as far north as western Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation ranged from 0 to 3.90, with the higher precipitation amounts occurring over the east half of the county. This was as much as an inch above normal for some locations in the eastern half of the county. Over the west half the precipitation was close to 1.70 below normal for the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total precipitation for the month across the county ranged from 0 to 2.50, with the higher precipitation amounts occurring over the east part of the county.  These precipitation amounts were 0.83 to 1.90 below normal for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally cold air filtered into western Colorado in the wake of an arctic cold front. While clouds generally kept temperatures above freezing for much of the region, enough clearing occurred across the northern valleys which resulted in strong radiational cooling and freezing temperatures overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 26 to 32 degrees with a low temperature of 32 degrees recorded at the Rangely Airport.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of Colorado during the month of November. As a result, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of the western slope and worsened to the exceptional category in some areas by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought persisted across the far eastern portion of the zone through the end of November while the exceptional (D4) category continued across the remainder of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure and associated cold front trekked across western Colorado and produced moderate to heavy snowfall over the southwest and west-central mountains. Favorable southwesterly flow ahead of this system kept snow levels high before colder air brought levels down to valley floors during the latter half of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure and associated cold front trekked across western Colorado and produced moderate to heavy snowfall over the southwest and west-central mountains. After a week of heavy, wind blown snow across the Central Colorado Rockies where up to four feet of snow had fallen, back country recreationists unintentionally triggered a hard slab avalanche while riding motorized snow-bikes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hard slab avalanche was unintentionally triggered by 3 motorized snow-bikers following a week of heavy, wind blown snow in the South Fork of Dickson Creek, east of Red and White Mountain. The avalanche was 3 to 4 feet in depth, 600 feet wide and debris ran for about 300 vertical feet. The avalanche was described a medium-sized and produced enough force to injure, bury, or kill a person. All 3 of the snow-bikers were trapped or partially buried. Only 1 survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across the region and produced moderate snow over the northern and portions of the central Colorado mountains as well as the Central Colorado River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous disturbances embedded in a decaying atmospheric river produced moderate to heavy snow over the northwest and west-central Colorado mountains. Portions of the Park Range received over 3 feet of snow. Sustained wind speeds exceeded 20 MPH at times with gusts of up to 48 MPH recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 18 to 30 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts close to 40 inches estimated at the Tower SNOTEL site. Sustained wind speeds exceeded 20 MPH at times. Travel was severely impacted along Colorado Highway 40 with Rabbit Ears Pass being closed for a period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front combined with a tight pressure gradient, instability aloft, and favorable upper level dynamics moved across western Colorado. This system produced moderate snow over the northern and central mountains as well as gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 16.5 inches estimated at the Tower SNOTEL site. Sustained wind speeds exceeded 20 to 30 MPH for several hours which led to reduced visibility in blowing snow. This resulted in severe travel impacts along Colorado Highway 40 with Rabbit Ears Pass being closed for at least an hour.

While clouds generally kept temperatures above freezing for much of the region, enough clearing occurred across the northern valleys which resulted in strong radiational cooling and freezing temperatures overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system and associated cold front dropped south across the Northern Rockies and into the Four Corners. The low remained quasi-stationary for about a day before lifting northeast into the Plains. This system produced widespread showers of rain in the lower elevations and snow in the mountains, though the cold front dropped snow levels to approximately 7000 feet. Additionally, high winds led to not only reduced visibility in blowing snow, but also numerous downed trees and power outages in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant blowing and drifting snow occurred across the region as winds gusted up to 50 to 60 MPH. A trained spotter measured 6.6 inches of snow 32 miles west-northwest of Maybell. Additionally, numerous power poles were knocked down between the spotter's location and the Utah border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system and associated cold front dropped south across the Northern Rockies and into the Four Corners. The low remained quasi-stationary for about a day before lifting northeast into the Plains. This system produced widespread showers of rain in the lower elevations and snow in the mountains, though the cold front dropped snow levels to approximately 7000 feet. Additionally, high winds led to not only reduced visibility in blowing snow, but also numerous downed trees and power outages in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 15 inches estimated at the Upper Taylor SNOTEL site. CoCoRaHS observers in and around Crested Butte measured 5 to 8 inches of snow. Additionally, the wet, heavy snow and strong winds led to numerous downed power lines and trees in South Crested Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwesterly flow set up overhead in the wake of a departing system. This, combined with clearing skies and light winds, led to freezing temperatures across northwest Colorado. Some valleys saw their season-ending freeze with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 18 miles north of Grand Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Gulch Fire began at the end of July due to lightning 18 miles north of Grand Junction and grew to 139,007 acres before it was contained in mid September. The combination of drought-stressed vegetation, unseasonably hot weather and steep terrain led to weeks of active burning. Smoke columns were often visible from Grand Junction and the surrounding area as the wildfire exhibited extreme fire behavior. During the night of August 18, the fire grew quickly due to thunderstorm winds up to 40 mph for a three to four hour period. As a result, the fire increased by more than 30,000 acres that night. As of August 27, 2020 the Pine Gulch Fire became the largest wildfire in Colorado State history, surpassing the Hayman Fire that burned near Colorado Springs in the summer of 2002.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grizzly Creek Fire began in mid August from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs. It grew to a total of 32,631 acres before it was contained in mid December. As a result of the fire, major Interstate-70 was closed through Glenwood Canyon for more than a week which greatly impacted summer travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought status for these counties remained largely unchanged, except for the drought worsening to D3 for the far western part of Yuma County.  Heavy rainfall events occurred over the eastern parts of these counties, which lessened the drought over the eastern half.  Total precipitation for the month ranged from 0.35 to 6.44, with the departure from normal ranging from 2.67 below normal to 3.43 above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southwest corner of the county remained in D3 Drought, improving to D2 Drought over the western half, with the east half not in a drought.  Precipitation for the month ranged from 1.10 to 6.44.  The monthly precipitation ranged from 0.48 to 3.43 above normal.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included:  64 mph at Denver International Airport; 62 mph at Centennial Airport, and 60 mph near Cherry Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong surface heating resulted in marginal instability and, when paired with residual monsoonal moisture, isolated thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain. As these cells drifted into the valleys they began to collapse and produce severe thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 62 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Durango-La Plata County Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sand Creek Fire began in mid June due to lightning 20 miles northwest of Pagosa Springs. The fire grew to 107 acres before it was finally contained in early August after receiving monsoonal moisture in late July.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Irish Fire began in early August due to lightning 18 miles northwest of Greystone and grew to 100 acres before it was contained almost a week later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 1 mile east of the Utah/Colorado border between Brown's Park and Dinosaur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pots Fire began at the end of July due to lightning 1 mile east of the Utah/Colorado border between Brown's Park and Dinosaur. The fire grew to 235 acres before it was contained in early August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system and associated cold front dropped south across the Northern Rockies and into the Four Corners. The low remained quasi-stationary for about a day before lifting northeast into the Plains. This system produced widespread showers of rain in the lower elevations and snow in the mountains, though the cold front dropped snow levels to approximately 7000 feet. Additionally, high winds led to not only reduced visibility in blowing snow, but also numerous downed trees and power outages in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with 2 to 5 inches measured by CoCoRaHS observers below 8500 feet. A trained spotter measured 9 inches of snow near Trout Lake. Winds gusted up to 47 MPH at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating acted on residual midlevel moisture to produce isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms across the higher terrain. As these cells drifted into the valleys they began to collapse and produce severe thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of subtropical moisture advected north into the region underneath a ridge of high pressure. Residual moisture from previous days convection helped to prime the atmosphere which led to an uptick in shower and thunderstorm coverage across western Colorado. Some storms produced heavy rain which led to localized debris flows across some west-central counties, including over the Pine Gulch Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A debris flow occurred as a result of heavy rain over the northern portion of the Pine Gulch Fire burn scar about 5 miles west of the intersection of County Road 202 and County Road 204. The debris flow trapped a farmer who was able to get out with a backhoe. The farmer estimated about half an inch of rain fell while a nearby Cooperative Observer measured up to 0.65 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As high pressure remained centered over the Southern Plains, monsoonal moisture advected north across the western slope of Colorado. This led to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours, some of which produced severe wind gusts across northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 64 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Craig-Moffat Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed across western Colorado and provided enough instability to fuel isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the evening hours. Some storms produced severe wind gusts over portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport.

CZ_NAME: CANON CITY VICINITY / EASTERN FREMONT COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm affected the region on the 25th and 26th, bringing heavy snow to both the higher terrain and parts of the plains in southern Colorado. A brief period of freezing drizzle did occur prior to this snow, with a period of heavy snow then occurring. The highest amounts were observed across the southwest mountains with this event. Additionally, a much colder air mass provided well below normal temperatures across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8.6 inches. 1 East Southeast Canon City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Trapped low level moisture in the wake of a departing storm led to areas of dense fog in the Grand Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Grand Valley including at the Grand Junction Regional Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Trapped low level moisture led to areas of dense fog in the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed across the Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin including at the Montrose Regional Airport as visibility was reduced to less than one quarter of a mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche was triggered by falling ice that broke away from a large ice pillar. The snow and ice fell over a cliff, picking up more loose snow on the slope below.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system brought blizzard conditions to the northeast plains of Colorado.  Numerous roads closures were posted east of Interstate 25 and over the Palmer Divide.  Highways closings included portions of eastbound interstates 70 and 76 in eastern Colorado due to strong winds and whiteout conditions.  I-70 was also closed westbound into the mountains, due to heavy snow and numerous accidents.  ||In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals ranged from 12 to 23 inches.  Along the urban corridor, south and west of a line from Boulder to Denver International Airport, storm totals ranged form 5 to 11 inches.  Across portions of the northeast plains of Colorado, blizzard conditions were observed.  Storm totals ranged from 3 to 9 inches. |Peak wind gusts included:  63 mph in Akron, 56 mph in Limon, 51 mph in Fort Morgan and 49 mph in Greeley and at Denver International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  22.5 inches, 4 miles southeast of Pinecliffe; 22 inches at Aspen Springs; 21 inches near Crescent Village; 17 inches near Genesee; 16 inches, 4 miles south of Brookvale; 15 inches near Evergreen; 14.5 inches near Kittredge; 13.0 inches near Silver Plume; 12 inches near Bergen Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure which originated off the coast of Baja, California moved inland and lifted across the Four Corners region. Favorable southwesterly orographics led to moderate and periods of wet, heavy snow across the higher elevations of the Southwest San Juan Mountains as well as over the Grand Mesa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 18 inches estimated at the Spud Mountain SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and unseasonably moist low pressure system lifted across the Four Corners region. Favorable southerly flow during the onset of the event produced moderate to heavy snow over the southern mountains and some valleys. Once cooler air moved into the region during the latter half of the storm, snowfall began to favor the northern and central Colorado mountains. Wind gusts exceeded 45 MPH at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 45 MPH at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense winds downed a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm system brought blizzard conditions to the northeast plains of Colorado.  Numerous roads closures were posted east of Interstate 25 and over the Palmer Divide.  Highways closings included portions of eastbound interstates 70 and 76 in eastern Colorado due to strong winds and whiteout conditions.  I-70 was also closed westbound into the mountains, due to heavy snow and numerous accidents.  ||In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals ranged from 12 to 23 inches.  Along the urban corridor, south and west of a line from Boulder to Denver International Airport, storm totals ranged form 5 to 11 inches.  Across portions of the northeast plains of Colorado, blizzard conditions were observed.  Storm totals ranged from 3 to 9 inches. |Peak wind gusts included:  63 mph in Akron, 56 mph in Limon, 51 mph in Fort Morgan and 49 mph in Greeley and at Denver International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  23 inches, 4 miles northeast of Nederland;  18.5 inches, 3 miles west of Jamestown; 18 inches, 2 miles east of Estes Park; 11.5 inches, 6 miles west of Bellvue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously. The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front progressed across western Colorado and generally produced light snow across the region. However, instability associated with the front led to the development of a strong convective snow band which extended from the La Sal Mountains in southeast Utah to the West Elk Mountains in west-central Colorado. This band produced localized snowfall totals of up to a foot across portions of the West Elks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area, much of that within 12 hours or less.

An unseasonably moist Pacific shortwave trough lifted across western Colorado and produced scattered to numerous showers beginning the night of May 10th. A break in the clouds the morning of May 11th fueled the atmosphere's instability and allowed showers and thunderstorms to redevelop across the region. The stronger storms produced frequent lightning, heavy rain, gusty winds to 63 MPH, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced large hail estimated to be the size of quarters in Olathe.

Six EF-0 landspout tornadoes occurred in Weld County in the span of approximately one hour.   The tornadoes tracked in a southeast to northwest direction over open country in an area from 5 miles east of Greeley to just east of Eaton. Three lasted less than 10 minutes, and the longest lived tornado was on the ground for 25 minutes and had a track length of 8 miles. Three farmsteads were impacted with minor damage, and there were no injuries or deaths.

An unseasonably moist Pacific shortwave trough lifted across western Colorado and produced scattered to numerous showers beginning the night of May 10th. A break in the clouds the morning of May 11th fueled the atmosphere's instability and allowed showers and thunderstorms to redevelop across the region. The stronger storms produced frequent lightning, heavy rain, gusty winds to 63 MPH, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated to be around 50 MPH produced damage to an NWS employee's fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Pacific low pressure system slowly drifted across the western United States. Strong southwest flow set up ahead of the disturbance over western Colorado with widespread breezy conditions at the surface. Winds gusted up to 68 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sustained wind speed of 40 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Craig-Moffat Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry and windy conditions, combined with critically dry vegetation, led to an uncontrolled wildfire 30 miles northeast of Meeker that lasted for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wyman Fire began on April 30th, 30 miles northeast of Meeker. It grew to 455 acres before it was contained a few days later on May 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air continued to be advected into western Colorado in the wake of a departing Pacific trough. Clear skies paired with cold mid-level temperatures and light winds led to subfreezing temperatures across portions of west-central Colorado. These bitterly cold temperatures led to significant peach crop damage in the Grand Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 19 to 29 degrees with a low temperature of 19 degrees recorded at the Grand Junction Regional Airport. This set a new record low temperature for the Grand Junction Airport, breaking the previous record of 21 degrees. Peach and apricot farmers in Palisade, Colorado estimated that more than half of the peach crop got damaged. Some orchards saw 95% kill of fruit buds. The last time damage of this scale occurred was back in 1999. The Governor of Colorado requested a Declaration of Disaster from the USDA for the agricultural community on the Western Slope of Colorado as a result of the hard freeze damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of mid-April snowstorms dropped up to a foot of snow near Crested Butte, Colorado. From April 23 to 27, temperatures warmed above freezing during the day and dropped back below freezing at night with some melting of the snowpack. Weak layers underneath the new snowpack and the warmer temperatures resulted in unstable conditions. Backcountry skiers unintentionally triggered an avalanche along a 42�� north-northeast facing slope with scattered tree cover.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The avalanche began as a wet-loose avalanche triggered by a backcountry skier. The wet-loose avalanche was only a few inches deep, and spread to 150 feet wide at the widest point. The avalanche carried the skier downhill and over a cliff, triggering a second wet-loose avalanche that eventually pinned the skier against trees resulting in a fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous upper level disturbances passed over the Intermountain West in response to a strong 180 knot upper level jet pushing inland. This resulted in a prolonged period of unsettled northwesterly flow which produced moderate to heavy snow across the northern and central mountains as well as some of the valleys. Winds gusted up to 45 MPH at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 46 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat burst occurred in NW Yuma County. The local mesonet stations recorded rising temperatures with strong wind gusts. Damage from the event included displaced hay bales, a broken power pole, and a destroyed farm outbuilding occurred near Joes, Colorado. An AWOS station (K2V6) at the Yuma Municipal Airport observed sustained southerly winds at 38 mph gusting to 47 mph as pressure fell 4.7 millibars. The dewpoint also fell from 32 F to 4 F as the temperature rose 13 degrees from 64 F to 77F. The data observed is that of a classic Heat Burst signature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat burst occurred near Joes causing damage to an approximately 75 year old farm outbuilding, broken utility pole, and displaced hay bale. Winds estimated to 80 MPH based on damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extremely dry and windy conditions, combined with critically dry vegetation, led to an uncontrolled wildfire 30 miles northeast of Meeker that lasted for several days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wyman Fire began on April 30th, 30 miles northeast of Meeker. It grew to 455 acres before it was contained a few days later on May 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began near a vacant mobile home approximately 3 miles west of Meeker in Lion's Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lion Fire began on April 7th from unknown causes in Lion's Canyon, or approximately 3 miles west of Meeker. It grew to 229 acres before it was contained at the end of the following day. An unoccupied mobile home was destroyed in the fire, along with several outbuildings and multiple vehicles.

One backcountry skier was killed by an avalanche near Red Peak, in the Gore Range west of Silverthorne.||In the mountains and foothills, storm totals included:  30 inches near Jamestown; 27 inches near Nederland; 22 inches near Allenspark; 21 inches near St Mary's Glacier; 18 inches in Estes Park, 16 inches near Crescent Village and Deer Ridge; 15.5 inches near Aspen Springs, 15 inches near Pinecliffe; 12 inches near Berthoud and Genesee; 11.5 inches, 9 miles west-southwest of Rustic, with 4 to 10 inches across the north central mountains and the remainder of the foothills.  ||Along the urban corridor and adjacent plains, the heaviest snowfall occurred across southeast Larimer, eastern Boulder, northeast Jefferson and central Weld Counties. Storm totals included:  16.9 inches in Boulder, which helped to establish a new seasonal snowfall record for the city;  14.5 inches in Niwot;  14 inches in Ft. Collins; 13.5 inches in Frederick; 13 inches in Broomfield, 12 inches in Lafayette and near Longmont; 10.5 inches in northwest Arvada, with 10 inches in Erie, Louisville, and Westminster.  ||Across the rest of the urban corridor and northeast plains, storm totals included:  8 inches in Iliff and near Windsor; 7.5 inches near Akron; 7 inches in Brighton; 6.5 inches in Edgewater and Northglenn; 5.5 inches in Greeley and 7 miles northwest of Purcell; with 2 to 5 inches elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included: 13.5 inches in Frederick; 10 inches in Erie, 8 inches near Windsor;  7 inches in Brighton; 6 inches near Hudson, 5.5 inches in Greeley and 7 miles northwest of Purcell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous disturbances embedded in a decaying atmospheric river produced moderate to heavy snow over the northwest and west-central Colorado mountains. Portions of the Park Range received over 3 feet of snow. Sustained wind speeds exceeded 20 MPH at times with gusts of up to 48 MPH recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area, much of that falling within 12 hours. Sustained wind speeds exceeded 20 MPH at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance and associated surface cold front produced moderate snow over the mountains of western Colorado and some of the northern valleys. Additionally, gusty winds occurred with the passage of the front which led to wind gusts in excess of 50 MPH at times as well as reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted from 46 to 52 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several snow squall warnings were issued for far eastern Colorado due to brief periods of blowing snow leading to reduced visibilties associated with a heavy band of snow moving from north to south across northwest Kansas and far east-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 51 mph was measured at 3 miles east northeast of Idalia, Colorado at 1439Z. Prolonged snow squall conditions were presumed to have occurred based on wind observations, proximity to counties meeting criteria, and radar signatures. The snow squall conditions were due to a slow moving band of heavier snow that led to near-blizzard conditions for less than 3 hours.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jetstream coupled with a very deep fetch of Pacific moisture, produced a prolonged period of heavy snow and strong winds in the mountains.  Peak wind gusts above timberline ranged from 55 to 65 mph.  The winter storm made travel nearly impossible at times, including Interstate 70 between Georgetown and Vail Pass.  In the mountains, storm totals ranged from 2 to  4 1/2 feet.  A SNOTEL site near Rabbit Ears Pass measured 54.5 inches of snow.  Some of the other more impressive totals included:  41.5 inches, 11 miles south of Rabbit Ears Pass; 40 inches at Breckenridge and Loveland ski areas; 34 inches near Cameron Pass; 33 inches near Berthoud Summit and Copper Mountain; 30 inches and Eldora, Winter Park and 4 miles southeast of Mount Zirkel.  Moderate to heavy snowfall also impacted the Front Range Foothills and portions of the urban corridor.  Storm totals in the foothills ranged from 8 to 18 inches, with 2 to 7 inches in most areas along the urban corridor, near the foothills and Palmer Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  54.5 inches near Rabbit Ears Pass, 41.5 inches, 11 miles south of Rabbit Ears Pass and 30 inches near Mount Zirkel.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jetstream coupled with a very deep fetch of Pacific moisture, produced a prolonged period of heavy snow and strong winds in the mountains.  Peak wind gusts above timberline ranged from 55 to 65 mph.  The winter storm made travel nearly impossible at times, including Interstate 70 between Georgetown and Vail Pass.  In the mountains, storm totals ranged from 2 to  4 1/2 feet.  A SNOTEL site near Rabbit Ears Pass measured 54.5 inches of snow.  Some of the other more impressive totals included:  41.5 inches, 11 miles south of Rabbit Ears Pass; 40 inches at Breckenridge and Loveland ski areas; 34 inches near Cameron Pass; 33 inches near Berthoud Summit and Copper Mountain; 30 inches and Eldora, Winter Park and 4 miles southeast of Mount Zirkel.  Moderate to heavy snowfall also impacted the Front Range Foothills and portions of the urban corridor.  Storm totals in the foothills ranged from 8 to 18 inches, with 2 to 7 inches in most areas along the urban corridor, near the foothills and Palmer Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  10 inches near Cherry Hills Village, 7 inches at Centennial Airport, with 2 to 5 inches elsewhere.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough trekked across the northern Great Basin before lifting across western Colorado. Strong upper level support allowed moderate snow to fall over the central mountains with gusty winds at the surface resulting in hazardous travel conditions at times, particularly along I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 10 inches estimated at the Vail Mountain SNOTEL site. Winds gusted from 45 to 50 MPH at times with a peak gust of 67 MPH recorded at the Crown automated station which led to reduced visibility in blowing snow. This resulted in severe travel impacts along Interstate 70 due to whiteout conditions as a snow squall moved overhead. The Aspen Airport recorded visibility of less than 1/4 mile and a peak wind gust of 50 MPH with this squall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area.

Despite near normal precipitation occurring, the D3 Extreme Drought level continued for East Central Colorado.  Precipitation amounts for the month ranged from .01-1, with the highest amounts near an inch in northwest Yuma County.  Normal precipitation for the month ranged from .5-1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed precipitation for the month ranged from 0.10-1, which was near normal.  The lowest precipitation amounts occurred in the southern half of the county. Normal precipitation amounts are 0.50-1.  Key impacts:  vegetation more favorable for rapid fire growth, little to no soil moisture, the ground is more favorable for blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours of July 14th, scattered thunderstorms, including a few supercells, developed across eastern El Paso County. As this activity shifted eastward they began to intensify and grow upscale into a larger thunderstorm complex. A series of supercells moved through Bent, Prowers, and Baca Counties producing a large non-damaging tornado, large hail, and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Springfield AWOS mesaured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough trekked across the northern Great Basin before lifting across western Colorado. Strong upper level support allowed moderate snow to fall over the central mountains with gusty winds at the surface resulting in hazardous travel conditions at times, particularly along I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 17 inches estimated at the Tower SNOTEL site. Winds gusted from 45 to 55 MPH with the automated sensor at Storm Peak Laboratory exceeding 70 MPH at times.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 7500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system impacted southern Colorado from September 8th through the 10th. This system brought an end to the above normal warmth, and provided the region with winter like weather. Strong northerly winds, heavy snow across the mountains and mountain valleys, and a wintry mix of rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain across parts of the I-25 corridor were all observed during this time. Additionally, well below normal temperatures brought an end to the growing season over much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amount of 9.5 inches. 7 NW Cuchara.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the later afternoon and evening, a line of thunderstorms stretched across Washington and Logan counties in eastern Colorado. As the line slowly moved east, another line of storms developed along a boundary in far eastern Washington and Yuma counties. As the second line intensified, the storms produced 60 mph wind gusts and hail to the size of quarters in Yuma County. The two lines of storms merged in Yuma County to produce a cluster of storms that entered into southwest Nebraska and Northwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wray AWOS station measured a 60 mph wind gust associated with the core of a strong thunderstorm moving through Wray at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed in eastern Colorado during the late afternoon hours. One thunderstorm that moved over Yuma, CO produced 0.83 inches of heavy rain in 20 minutes causing flash flooding in town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yuma County Emergency Manager reported street flooding in town. A few garages were reported to have flood water in them as the water in the street was going over the curbs. The EM also reported 0.83 inches of heavy rain fell in 20 minutes in town that caused the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm affected the region on the 25th and 26th, bringing heavy snow to both the higher terrain and parts of the plains in southern Colorado. A brief period of freezing drizzle did occur prior to this snow, with a period of heavy snow then occurring. The highest amounts were observed across the southwest mountains with this event. Additionally, a much colder air mass provided well below normal temperatures across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 16.0 inches. 6 East Hillside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During October the drought worsened and expanded in coverage for East Central Colorado.  By the end of October the D3 Extreme Drought covered almost all of Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties.  Precipitation for the month ranged from 0.25 to 1, which was half an inch to an inch below normal.  Normal precipitation amounts range from half an inch to an inch, with part of Yuma County having as much as two inches of precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed precipitation for the month ranged from a quarter to half an inch.  Normal precipitation amounts ranged from half an inch to an inch and a half.  This is a departure of half an inch to an inch from normal.  D3 Extreme Drought encompassed all of the county.

CZ_NAME: COLORADO SPRINGS VICINITY / SOUTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent winter storm affected the region on the 25th and 26th, bringing heavy snow to both the higher terrain and parts of the plains in southern Colorado. A brief period of freezing drizzle did occur prior to this snow, with a period of heavy snow then occurring. The highest amounts were observed across the southwest mountains with this event. Additionally, a much colder air mass provided well below normal temperatures across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8.9 inches. 4 South Air Force Academy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and hail up to golfball size over northern Lincoln County.  Law enforcement reported that several vehicles sustained broken windshields along Interstate 70.  Thunderstorms winds up to 70 mph snapped tree branches and toppled at least one tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds snapped branches and knocked down one tree, approximately 12 inches in diameter and 30 feet tall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, from quarter to tennis ball size over Adams, Lincoln and Weld counties.  The hail damaged trees and property, including multiple vehicles. One tornado touched down briefly in open country.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds across parts of Arapahoe, Jefferson, Morgan, Washington and Weld counties. Peak wind gusts up to 70 mph knocked down some trees.  Hail up to golf ball size was also reported.

During the later afternoon and evening, a line of thunderstorms stretched across Washington and Logan counties in eastern Colorado. As the line slowly moved east, another line of storms developed along a boundary in far eastern Washington and Yuma counties. As the second line intensified, the storms produced 60 mph wind gusts and hail to the size of quarters in Yuma County. The two lines of storms merged in Yuma County to produce a cluster of storms that entered into southwest Nebraska and Northwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny sized hail was reported at the location via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A worsening drought that started in the spring of 2020, continued to burn through October.  The month of August was the warmest on record, and in the middle of one of the warmest summers on record.  The combination of hot and dry conditions, in addition to critically dry fuels from years of beetle kill, resulted in a massive wildfire.  The Cameron Peak fire, the largest wildfire in the state's history started on August 13th, and consumed 23,021 acres through the month of August.  It continued to burn well into fall, and consumed nearly 210,000 acres of forestland by late October.  A total of 441 buildings were destroyed, including:  213 residences, 211 outbuildings, and 17 buildings associated with Shambala Mountain Center.  It resulted in extensive voluntary and mandatory evacuations and numerous road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cameron Peak wildfire became the largest in state history.  It started in a thick forest with very steep terrain.  In the month of August, the fire had consumed 23,021 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During August the drought status for Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties improved slightly, with the coverage of D3 Extreme Drought Status decreasing over these counties due to above normal rainfall occurring.  However, the D3 status was still over roughly the southwest half of Cheyenne County and part of the western half of Yuma County.  D2 Severe Drought Status covered the rest of Cheyenne County, and the western half of Kit Carson and Yuma counties.  Average rainfall for the month for these counties ranged from 0.02 to 0.52.  Total monthly rainfall ranged from 0.40 to 4.31, with the high rainfall amounts tied to the Wray area due to heavy rainfall occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county continued to be in D2 to D3 Drought Status.  Total precipitation for the month ranged from 0.43-1.75, with the higher precipitation amounts occurring over the eastern part of the county.  Average rainfall amounts ranged from 0.02-0.28.

This system brought an end to the above normal warmth, and provided the region with winter like weather. Strong northerly winds, heavy snow across the mountains and mountain valleys, and a wintry mix of rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain across parts of the I-25 corridor were all observed during this time. Additionally, well below normal temperatures brought an end to the growing season over much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amount of 7.1 inches. 1 SSE Crestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high winds sustained at 20-50 mph with gusts ranging from 50 to 72 mph. These high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to approximately 100 yards across eastern Colorado. As the front moved across Yuma County, the blowing dust occurred along the cold front with the strongest winds occurring behind the front. As the cold front progressed across Kit Carson County, the high winds caught up, so that the high winds and blowing dust were occurring along the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced high wind gusts in southeastern Yuma County from 50 to 58 mph. The 58 mph wind gust was observed by a station in Hale, CO. These high winds caused blowing dust in the same part of the county, which reduced visibilities to less than 50 feet at times. Reduced visibilities from seven to two miles were also observed via AWOS stations in Yuma and Wray, with 41-53 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high winds sustained at 20-50 mph with gusts ranging from 50 to 72 mph. These high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to approximately 100 yards across eastern Colorado. As the front moved across Yuma County, the blowing dust occurred along the cold front with the strongest winds occurring behind the front. As the cold front progressed across Kit Carson County, the high winds caught up, so that the high winds and blowing dust were occurring along the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across Kit Carson County, producing wind gusts up to 60 mph. The strong wind gusts lifted dust high into the air, which reduced visibilities to less than one block in Flagler and between one to three-quarters of a mile in Burlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm high winds associated with a passing cold front occurred in eastcentral Colorado during the afternoon hours of November 14th, 2020. The maximum sustained wind speed recorded was 47 mph and the maximum wind gust speed recorded was 63 mph. These high winds also caused reduced visibility down to a quarter mile due to blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds between 40-47 mph and wind gusts up to 63 mph were reported across the county. Mesonet station 2 miles northeast of Vernon, CO reported the max wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system impacted southern Colorado from September 8th through the 10th. This system brought an end to the above normal warmth, and provided the region with winter like weather. Strong northerly winds, heavy snow across the mountains and mountain valleys, and a wintry mix of rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain across parts of the I-25 corridor were all observed during this time. Additionally, well below normal temperatures brought an end to the growing season over much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 12 inches of snow. 3 SSE Monte Vista.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Drought status for East Central Colorado changed little during the month.  The D2 Severe Drought expanded to cover the west half of Yuma County, all of Kit Carson County, and the rest of Cheyenne County.  D3 Extreme Drought remained unchanged, covering the southwest half of Cheyenne County.  Observed precipitation for the month ranged from 0.25 - 1.5, with the lowest precipitation amounts occurring over Cheyenne County and the highest over northern Yuma County.

Precipitation for the month was up to 1 below normal, with the lowest amounts below normal in the eastern part of the county.  D2 Drought expanded across the east half of the county during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During October the drought worsened and expanded in coverage for East Central Colorado.  By the end of October the D3 Extreme Drought covered almost all of Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties.  Precipitation for the month ranged from 0.25 to 1, which was half an inch to an inch below normal.  Normal precipitation amounts range from half an inch to an inch, with part of Yuma County having as much as two inches of precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed precipitation for the month ranged from a quarter inch to one inch. Normal precipitation amounts ranged from an inch to an inch and a half. This is a departure of half an inch to an inch from normal.  By the end of the month D3 Extreme Drought encompassed all of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete supercells formed in eastern Colorado and moved south across Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties. As the discrete cells moved south, more cells developed in far northeast Colorado and southwest Nebraska forming a cluster of supercell that mainly impacted Yuma County. The cells during this event produced various sized hail up to hen egg in size across Yuma, Kit Carson, and Cheyenne counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various sized hail was reported from quarter to ping pong ball in size fell for a duration of approximately 11 minutes.

Isolated thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain early in the afternoon, and then moved east into the plains in the late afternoon and evening producing pockets of severe weather with heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser on CO-10 southwest of La Junta captured a brief tornado over open fields on Sunday evening. Video shows a quickly descending funnel reaching the ground and then quickly becoming enveloped by sheets of rain. The tornado lasted no longer than 2-minutes, per chasers video, or at least before the sheets of rain obscured the view.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high winds sustained at 20-50 mph with gusts ranging from 50 to 72 mph. These high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to approximately 100 yards across eastern Colorado. As the front moved across Yuma County, the blowing dust occurred along the cold front with the strongest winds occurring behind the front. As the cold front progressed across Kit Carson County, the high winds caught up, so that the high winds and blowing dust were occurring along the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front caused wind gusts ranging from 50 to 72 mph across Cheyenne County. These strong winds lifted dust into the air, reducing visibility between one-quarter mile and approximately one hundred yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southeastward cold front produced high winds sustained at 20-50 mph with gusts ranging from 50 to 72 mph. These high winds produced blowing dust with visibilities reduced to approximately 100 yards across eastern Colorado. As the front moved across Yuma County, the blowing dust occurred along the cold front with the strongest winds occurring behind the front. As the cold front progressed across Kit Carson County, the high winds caught up, so that the high winds and blowing dust were occurring along the frontal boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced high winds ranging from 52 mph in Flagler, CO to a peak of 60 mph in Burlington, CO. These strong winds caused blowing dust, which reduced visibilities to less than one block in Flagler and between one to three-quarters of a mile in Burlington.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports received of various sized hail from dime to penny in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During August the drought status for Kit Carson and Cheyenne counties improved slightly, with the coverage of D3 Extreme Drought Status decreasing over these counties due to above normal rainfall occurring.  However, the D3 status was still over roughly the southwest half of Cheyenne County and part of the western half of Yuma County.  D2 Severe Drought Status covered the rest of Cheyenne County, and the western half of Kit Carson and Yuma counties.  Average rainfall for the month for these counties ranged from 0.02 to 0.52.  Total monthly rainfall ranged from 0.40 to 4.31, with the high rainfall amounts tied to the Wray area due to heavy rainfall occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought status changed little through the month, with D2 and D3 Drought Status over the western half of the county.  Total precipitation for the month ranged from 0.40-4.31, with the highest amounts occurring over the Wray area due to heavy rainfall.  The average precipitation for the month ranged from 0.10 to 0.50.

CZ_NAME: WESTCLIFFE VICINITY / WET MOUNTAIN VALLEY BELOW 8500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system moved through the region, providing a favorable setup for widespread snow across southern Colorado. Highest snowfall totals were across the higher terrain, and parts of the far eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow of 5.8 inches. 5 west Westcliffe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred across areas of Northeast Colorado during the morning of October 25th followed by snow during the evening, which lasted into the afternoon of October 26th. This led to several vehicle accidents during this time.  Snow amounts ranged from three to seven inches, with the highest amount occurring in central Yuma County.  A quarter of an inch of ice was reported south of Burlington, and an 1/8 of an inch of ice was reported in Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm snowfall total amounts across Yuma county ranged from 2.5 inches to 7.2 inches. 7.2 inches was reported at 3.6 miles NNE Vernon. An eighth of an inch of ice was reported in the county as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced very strong wind across Elbert, Lincoln and Washington counties.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 58 mph to 64 mph.

A cold front moved south across the region during the late afternoon and evening, providing a period of high northerly winds to southern Colorado. Multiple wind gust reports in excess of 60 mph were noted, that helped develop a dust storm, or haboob. As this dust storm moved through portions of the plains, visibility was reduced to near zero at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wall of dust moved through and reduced visibility to a few feet. 5 E Las Animas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.

A strong storm system moved across the area the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  This system produced severe thunderstorms and high winds, across northeast Colorado, with snow across the mountains, valley areas and higher foothills.  In addition rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred across the plains.   Storm totals included:  15 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 7 miles northwest of Red Feather Lakes; 11 inches near Cameron Pass; with 4 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Strong northwest winds developed across the northeast plains on the evening of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th, with peak gusts from 58 to 71 mph.  ||Severe thunderstorms  on the evening of the 8th, produced powerful and destructive wind gusts along with nickel to quarter size hail, over portions of northeast Colorado.  Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron.  The swath of damaging winds extended from central Washington County into Logan County and across western areas of Phillips and Sedgwick counties.  Widespread crop and property damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of Colorado during the month of August. As a result, drought conditions in southwest and west-central Colorado either persisted or worsened with the drought expanding north to encompass much of the western slope by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought continued across the eastern and central portions of the zone and expanded to include the entirety of the forecast zone by the end of August.

A storm system brought snow and blowing snow to the northern mountains, with strong post frontal bora winds to the Front Range Foothills, I-25 corridor and northeast plains.  Peak wind gusts from 58 to 85 mph were recorded across the region.  A few trees were reportedly knocked down as well.   In addition to the strong winds, storm totals in the mountains ranged from 3 to 9 inches.  ||In and near the Front Range mountains and foothills, the more impressive gusts included:  91 mph near Loveland Pass; 85 mph 3 miles south of Boulder, 80 mph, 3 miles northwest of Boulder; 79 mph near Marshall, 78 mph near Glen Haven and 75 mph near Rocky Flats.  Across the northeast plains of Colorado, the stronger gusts included:  76 mph in Fort Morgan, 70 mph at Cedar Point; 69 mph at Akron; 68 mph, 11 miles northwest of Westplains, and 67 mph near Crook and 8 miles south of Holyoke.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included 61 mph, 4 miles east-northeast of Severance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that produced up to 70 mph wind gusts and large hail up to tennis ball in size across Yuma County. Supercells developed over Cheyenne County as the northern cluster crossed into Kansas and Nebraska. The southern cells eventually formed a cluster that was able to produce up to half dollar sized hail, a landspout, and caused a portion of Highway 385 to become impassable due to flash flooding.

The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the area the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  This system produced severe thunderstorms and high winds, across northeast Colorado, with snow across the mountains, valley areas and higher foothills.  In addition rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred across the plains.   Storm totals included:  15 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 7 miles northwest of Red Feather Lakes; 11 inches near Cameron Pass; with 4 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Strong northwest winds developed across the northeast plains on the evening of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th, with peak gusts from 58 to 71 mph.  ||Severe thunderstorms  on the evening of the 8th, produced powerful and destructive wind gusts along with nickel to quarter size hail, over portions of northeast Colorado.  Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron.  The swath of damaging winds extended from central Washington County into Logan County and across western areas of Phillips and Sedgwick counties.  Widespread crop and property damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Macroburst winds caused widespread damage throughout the city of Akron. Trees were downed and branches were broken on every street. On the south side of town, several metal buildings were stripped of their roofs and siding. Agricultural structures along the tracks were destroyed: a grain bin collapsed and part of a grain elevator structure collapsed on a tractor trailer parked beneath. The Akron fairgrounds sustained roof damage, and a historic Ferris wheel was folded over. Damage was in the EF0-EF1 range with estimated 80-100 mph wind.

Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that produced up to 70 mph wind gusts and large hail up to tennis ball in size across Yuma County. Supercells developed over Cheyenne County as the northern cluster crossed into Kansas and Nebraska. The southern cells eventually formed a cluster that was able to produce up to half dollar sized hail, a landspout, and caused a portion of Highway 385 to become impassable due to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement and a trained storm spotter provided several reports of several inches of water flowing across Hwy 385 at mile marker 147 south of Cheyenne Wells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary thunderstorm producing prolonged heavy rainfall over Dundy County Nebraska began to develop west into Yuma County during the night.  The heavy rainfall continued into the overnight hours before ending.  Up to six inches of rainfall occurred, and was centered over Highway 34, with amounts declining a few miles west of Wray.  The axis of heavy rainfall was also centered over the Republican River Basin, which caused flooding in Wray well after the storm had ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the heavy rainfall expanded west, so did the flooding.  Highway 34 was closed from the state line west to Wray due to flooding.  The ball fields on the east side of Wray were also flooded. There was plenty of erosion/damage along the edges of the Republican River. There were also lots of basements flooded (around 15-20 significantly). Damage from the flood waters is unknown at this time. One thing the Wray City Manager wanted to be noted was that there are 6 flood control dams on the south side of the town. These worked perfectly and slowed the water. Had the system failed, the flooding damage would have been much worse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought snow and blowing snow to the northern mountains, with strong post frontal bora winds to the Front Range Foothills, I-25 corridor and northeast plains.  Peak wind gusts from 58 to 85 mph were recorded across the region.  A few trees were reportedly knocked down as well.   In addition to the strong winds, storm totals in the mountains ranged from 3 to 9 inches.  ||In and near the Front Range mountains and foothills, the more impressive gusts included:  91 mph near Loveland Pass; 85 mph 3 miles south of Boulder, 80 mph, 3 miles northwest of Boulder; 79 mph near Marshall, 78 mph near Glen Haven and 75 mph near Rocky Flats.  Across the northeast plains of Colorado, the stronger gusts included:  76 mph in Fort Morgan, 70 mph at Cedar Point; 69 mph at Akron; 68 mph, 11 miles northwest of Westplains, and 67 mph near Crook and 8 miles south of Holyoke.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included 67 mph at Crook and 59 mph, 2 miles east-southeast of Sterling.

The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.

A strong storm system moved across the area the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  This system produced severe thunderstorms and high winds, across northeast Colorado, with snow across the mountains, valley areas and higher foothills.  In addition rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred across the plains.   Storm totals included:  15 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 7 miles northwest of Red Feather Lakes; 11 inches near Cameron Pass; with 4 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Strong northwest winds developed across the northeast plains on the evening of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th, with peak gusts from 58 to 71 mph.  ||Severe thunderstorms  on the evening of the 8th, produced powerful and destructive wind gusts along with nickel to quarter size hail, over portions of northeast Colorado.  Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron.  The swath of damaging winds extended from central Washington County into Logan County and across western areas of Phillips and Sedgwick counties.  Widespread crop and property damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included 64 mph at Fort Morgan Airport and 58 mph, 2 miles east-northeast of Wiggins.

A storm system brought snow and blowing snow to the northern mountains, with strong post frontal bora winds to the Front Range Foothills, I-25 corridor and northeast plains.  Peak wind gusts from 58 to 85 mph were recorded across the region.  A few trees were reportedly knocked down as well.   In addition to the strong winds, storm totals in the mountains ranged from 3 to 9 inches.  ||In and near the Front Range mountains and foothills, the more impressive gusts included:  91 mph near Loveland Pass; 85 mph 3 miles south of Boulder, 80 mph, 3 miles northwest of Boulder; 79 mph near Marshall, 78 mph near Glen Haven and 75 mph near Rocky Flats.  Across the northeast plains of Colorado, the stronger gusts included:  76 mph in Fort Morgan, 70 mph at Cedar Point; 69 mph at Akron; 68 mph, 11 miles northwest of Westplains, and 67 mph near Crook and 8 miles south of Holyoke.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included:  76 mph in Fort Morgan, 62 mph at Fort Morgan Airport and 59 mph, 2 miles east-northeast of Wiggins.

The drought status for these counties remained largely unchanged, except for the drought worsening to D3 for the far western part of Yuma County.  Heavy rainfall events occurred over the eastern parts of these counties, which lessened the drought over the eastern half.  Total precipitation for the month ranged from 0.35 to 6.44, with the departure from normal ranging from 2.67 below normal to 3.43 above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western part of the county worsened from D2 to D3 Drought, with the eastern half remaining in D1 Drought.  The precipitation ranged from 0.35 to 5.78.  The monthly precipitation ranged from 2.67 below normal to 2.78 above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that produced up to 70 mph wind gusts and large hail up to tennis ball in size across Yuma County. Supercells developed over Cheyenne County as the northern cluster crossed into Kansas and Nebraska. The southern cells eventually formed a cluster that was able to produce up to half dollar sized hail, a landspout, and caused a portion of Highway 385 to become impassable due to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported a large, dead tree blown over along with numerous tree limbs down on the property due to strong winds associated with a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms formed over the Colorado High Plains increasing in coverage and intensity as they moved east across eastern Colorado. In Colorado, the storms produced up to golf ball sized hail and two landspout tornadoes across Yuma and Kit Carson Counties.  Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding to occur over CR 54 less than a mile north of CR N.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail stripped some grain crops on the property.

In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense winds snapped tree limbs up to 6 inches in diameter.

A strong storm system moved across the area the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  This system produced severe thunderstorms and high winds, across northeast Colorado, with snow across the mountains, valley areas and higher foothills.  In addition rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred across the plains.   Storm totals included:  15 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 7 miles northwest of Red Feather Lakes; 11 inches near Cameron Pass; with 4 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Strong northwest winds developed across the northeast plains on the evening of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th, with peak gusts from 58 to 71 mph.  ||Severe thunderstorms  on the evening of the 8th, produced powerful and destructive wind gusts along with nickel to quarter size hail, over portions of northeast Colorado.  Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron.  The swath of damaging winds extended from central Washington County into Logan County and across western areas of Phillips and Sedgwick counties.  Widespread crop and property damage was observed.

Despite near normal precipitation occurring, the D3 Extreme Drought level continued for East Central Colorado.  Precipitation amounts for the month ranged from .01-1, with the highest amounts near an inch in northwest Yuma County.  Normal precipitation for the month ranged from .5-1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed precipitation for the month ranged from 0.01-0.25, which was near normal.  The lowest precipitation amounts occurred in the southeast part of the county.  Normal precipitation amounts are 0.50-1.  Key impacts:  vegetation more favorable for rapid fire growth, little to no soil moisture, the ground is more favorable for blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms formed over the Colorado High Plains increasing in coverage and intensity as they moved east across eastern Colorado. In Colorado, the storms produced up to golf ball sized hail and two landspout tornadoes across Yuma and Kit Carson Counties.  Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding to occur over CR 54 less than a mile north of CR N.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small roping (landspout) tornado reported. Estimated near MM412 of I-70. Evaluation of radar imagery indicates that most likely boundary interaction was southeast of MM412 of I-70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that produced up to 70 mph wind gusts and large hail up to tennis ball in size across Yuma County. Supercells developed over Cheyenne County as the northern cluster crossed into Kansas and Nebraska. The southern cells eventually formed a cluster that was able to produce up to half dollar sized hail, a landspout, and caused a portion of Highway 385 to become impassable due to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a couple different reports of landspouts around this time. However, based on radar imagery and pictures/videos received, it appears that one single longer-lasting landspout tornado occurred. Reports described the times accurately and indicated the tornado occurred for around 10 minutes in duration, remaining nearly stationary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwesterly flow set up overhead in the wake of a departing system. This, combined with clearing skies and light winds, led to freezing temperatures across northwest Colorado. Some valleys saw their season-ending freeze with this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 21 to 28 degrees with a low temperature of 21 degrees recorded at the Craig-Moffat Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of the 8th, light snow developed over Eastern Colorado, gradually spreading east through the night and into the following morning.  Snowfall amounts ranged from a trace to as much as six inches, with the higher amounts in far southwest Yuma County and northwest Kit Carson County where the band of snow was the strongest.  Snowfall amounts generally declined to the east to less than an inch east of the Colorado border.  The snow changed to rain during the morning before ending.  This snow event broke the record for the earliest snowfall of the season.  Prior records for the first snow of the season ranged from the 11th to the 27th of September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts for the county were around an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense winds uprooted or snapped trees.
