EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system and associated cold front dropped south across the Northern Rockies and into the Four Corners. The low remained quasi-stationary for about a day before lifting northeast into the Plains. This system produced widespread showers of rain in the lower elevations and snow in the mountains, though the cold front dropped snow levels to approximately 7000 feet. Additionally, high winds led to not only reduced visibility in blowing snow, but also numerous downed trees and power outages in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area. A peak wind gust of 56 MPH occurred at the Gunnison-Crested Butte Regional Airport. These strong winds led to numerous downed trees and power outages in Gunnison County, as reported by the Emergency Manager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours in eastern Colorado due to an upper air disturbance. These thunderstorms yielded winds up to 59 mph that damaged property. As the showers and storms diminished in the early hours of the next day, a wind gust of 58 mph associated with a heat burst was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was recorded by K2V6 AWOS at location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station at location recorded a thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures of damaged farm buildings received via social media. Damage seen included snapped wooden posts, debris driven into a metal grain bin, and a building with a tin roof partially stripped and an awning partially collapsed. Wind speed estimated based on storm damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed during the evening hours in eastern Colorado which moved eastward toward the border. Thunderstorms yielded wind gusts up to 66 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station recorded a 66 mph thunderstorm wind gust at location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Drought status for East Central Colorado changed little during the month.  The D2 Severe Drought expanded to cover the west half of Yuma County, all of Kit Carson County, and the rest of Cheyenne County.  D3 Extreme Drought remained unchanged, covering the southwest half of Cheyenne County.  Observed precipitation for the month ranged from 0.25 - 1.5, with the lowest precipitation amounts occurring over Cheyenne County and the highest over northern Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation for the county ranged from 0.25 to 1, with most of the county in the 0.25 to 0.50 range.  These precipitation amounts were 0.5 to 1 below normal.  D3 Drought was in place over the southwest half of the county, with D2 Drought elsewhere.

In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.

Strong to high winds occurred over Cheyenne County in Colorado as a trough moved slowly across eastern Colorado. A ridge over the eastern United States blocked the low from moving from the western half of the country, which allowed the winds to occur at an unusual time late Saturday night and extend into early Sunday morning for a duration of seven hours. In Cheyenne County, the winds were sustained up to 40-60 mph with a peak gust of 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Firstview mesonet station observed 7 hours of high winds sustained up to 40-60 mph with gusts ranging from 50-75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system and associated cold front dropped south across the Northern Rockies and into the Four Corners. Gusty winds accompanied the front which led to numerous downed trees and power lines across the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted from 50 to 70 MPH in and around Steamboat Springs. These high winds led to numerous downed trees and power lines in Routt County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abnormally strong and cold low pressure system and associated cold front dropped south across the Northern Rockies and into the Four Corners. The low remained quasi-stationary for about a day before lifting northeast into the Plains. This system produced widespread showers of rain in the lower elevations and snow in the mountains, though the cold front dropped snow levels to approximately 7000 feet. Additionally, high winds led to not only reduced visibility in blowing snow, but also numerous downed trees and power outages in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant blowing and drifting snow occurred across the region as winds gusted up to 63 MPH at times. The strong winds also led to reduced visibilities of a quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that produced up to 70 mph wind gusts and large hail up to tennis ball in size across Yuma County. Supercells developed over Cheyenne County as the northern cluster crossed into Kansas and Nebraska. The southern cells eventually formed a cluster that was able to produce up to half dollar sized hail, a landspout, and caused a portion of Highway 385 to become impassable due to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter observed pea to nickel sized hail.

CZ_NAME: DEL NORTE VICINITY / NORTHERN SAN LUIS VALLEY BELOW 8500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system impacted southern Colorado from September 8th through the 10th. This system brought an end to the above normal warmth, and provided the region with winter like weather. Strong northerly winds, heavy snow across the mountains and mountain valleys, and a wintry mix of rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain across parts of the I-25 corridor were all observed during this time. Additionally, well below normal temperatures brought an end to the growing season over much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 16 inches of snow. Del Norte.

A strong storm system moved across the area the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  This system produced severe thunderstorms and high winds, across northeast Colorado, with snow across the mountains, valley areas and higher foothills.  In addition rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred across the plains.   Storm totals included:  15 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 7 miles northwest of Red Feather Lakes; 11 inches near Cameron Pass; with 4 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Strong northwest winds developed across the northeast plains on the evening of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th, with peak gusts from 58 to 71 mph.  ||Severe thunderstorms  on the evening of the 8th, produced powerful and destructive wind gusts along with nickel to quarter size hail, over portions of northeast Colorado.  Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron.  The swath of damaging winds extended from central Washington County into Logan County and across western areas of Phillips and Sedgwick counties.  Widespread crop and property damage was observed.

The drought status for these counties remained largely unchanged, except for the drought worsening to D3 for the far western part of Yuma County.  Heavy rainfall events occurred over the eastern parts of these counties, which lessened the drought over the eastern half.  Total precipitation for the month ranged from 0.35 to 6.44, with the departure from normal ranging from 2.67 below normal to 3.43 above normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D3 Drought was in place for most of the county.  The precipitation ranged from 1.42 to 5.59.  The monthly precipitation ranged from 1 below normal to 2.76 above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms formed over the Colorado High Plains increasing in coverage and intensity as they moved east across eastern Colorado. In Colorado, the storms produced up to golf ball sized hail and two landspout tornadoes across Yuma and Kit Carson Counties.  Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding to occur over CR 54 less than a mile north of CR N.

CZ_NAME: N & NE ELBERT COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / N LINCOLN COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought snow and blowing snow to the northern mountains, with strong post frontal bora winds to the Front Range Foothills, I-25 corridor and northeast plains.  Peak wind gusts from 58 to 85 mph were recorded across the region.  A few trees were reportedly knocked down as well.   In addition to the strong winds, storm totals in the mountains ranged from 3 to 9 inches.  ||In and near the Front Range mountains and foothills, the more impressive gusts included:  91 mph near Loveland Pass; 85 mph 3 miles south of Boulder, 80 mph, 3 miles northwest of Boulder; 79 mph near Marshall, 78 mph near Glen Haven and 75 mph near Rocky Flats.  Across the northeast plains of Colorado, the stronger gusts included:  76 mph in Fort Morgan, 70 mph at Cedar Point; 69 mph at Akron; 68 mph, 11 miles northwest of Westplains, and 67 mph near Crook and 8 miles south of Holyoke.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included:  70 mph, 3 miles south of Cedar Point; 66 mph at Limon Municipal Airport and 62 mph at Hugo.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS BETWEEN 8500 & 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system impacted southern Colorado from September 8th through the 10th. This system brought an end to the above normal warmth, and provided the region with winter like weather. Strong northerly winds, heavy snow across the mountains and mountain valleys, and a wintry mix of rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain across parts of the I-25 corridor were all observed during this time. Additionally, well below normal temperatures brought an end to the growing season over much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 13.1 inches of snow. 6 W Westcliffe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south across the region during the late afternoon and evening, providing a period of high northerly winds to southern Colorado. Multiple wind gust reports in excess of 60 mph were noted, that helped develop a dust storm, or haboob. As this dust storm moved through portions of the plains, visibility was reduced to near zero at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approaching wall of dust. Towner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that produced up to 70 mph wind gusts and large hail up to tennis ball in size across Yuma County. Supercells developed over Cheyenne County as the northern cluster crossed into Kansas and Nebraska. The southern cells eventually formed a cluster that was able to produce up to half dollar sized hail, a landspout, and caused a portion of Highway 385 to become impassable due to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public citizen observed hail up to tennis ball in size. Most of the hail was nickel to quarter in size. As hail fell at the location, winds were estimated to have 40 mph gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of Colorado during the month of September. As a result, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of the western slope and worsened to the exceptional category in some areas by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought persisted across the western and eastern portions of the zone and worsened to the exceptional (D4) category in the central portion of the zone by the end of September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grizzly Creek Fire began in mid August from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs. It grew to a total of 32,631 acres before it was contained in mid December. As a result of the fire, major Interstate-70 was closed through Glenwood Canyon for more than a week which greatly impacted summer travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.

A strong storm system moved across the area the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  This system produced severe thunderstorms and high winds, across northeast Colorado, with snow across the mountains, valley areas and higher foothills.  In addition rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred across the plains.   Storm totals included:  15 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 7 miles northwest of Red Feather Lakes; 11 inches near Cameron Pass; with 4 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Strong northwest winds developed across the northeast plains on the evening of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th, with peak gusts from 58 to 71 mph.  ||Severe thunderstorms  on the evening of the 8th, produced powerful and destructive wind gusts along with nickel to quarter size hail, over portions of northeast Colorado.  Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron.  The swath of damaging winds extended from central Washington County into Logan County and across western areas of Phillips and Sedgwick counties.  Widespread crop and property damage was observed.

Supercell thunderstorms formed over the Colorado High Plains increasing in coverage and intensity as they moved east across eastern Colorado. In Colorado, the storms produced up to golf ball sized hail and two landspout tornadoes across Yuma and Kit Carson Counties.  Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding to occur over CR 54 less than a mile north of CR N.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought a period of moderate to heavy snowfall to areas in and near the Front Range mountains and foothills.  Storm totals in the mountains generally ranged from 5 to 10 inches, with one station reporting 14 inches.  In foothills of Boulder and Jefferson counties, as well as Denver and some of the western suburbs, storms totals ranged from 2 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storms totals included 7 inches at Joe Wright, with 4.5 inches 4 miles southeast of Gould.

Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated along a dry line in central Colorado moving east and forming a cluster of supercells that produced up to 70 mph wind gusts and large hail up to tennis ball in size across Yuma County. This led to several vehicle accidents during this time.  Snow amounts ranged from three to seven inches, with the highest amount occurring in central Yuma County.  A quarter of an inch of ice was reported south of Burlington, and an 1/8 of an inch of ice was reported in Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts ranged between 4-6 inches across Kit Carson county. Six inches of snow and 0.25 inches of ice was reported 5 miles south of Burlington, CO.||Seventeen slide-off accidents were reported across Kit Carson county on Oct. 25th and Oct. 26th due to hazardous driving conditions due to freezing rain and snow. Accidents occurred near the CO towns of Flagler, Seibert, Stratton, and Burlington. There were no known injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low brought an end to record heat through the first week of September and provided Denver with one of its earliest measurable snowfalls on record. By the evening of September 7th, a series of cold fronts progressed southward from Wyoming into Colorado, and dropped the temperature down into the low 30s by early morning of the 8th. Snow developed across the Front Range mountains and foothills, while a mix of rain and snow developed along the I-25 corridor.  On the 8th, accumulating snow was mostly confined to the higher elevations, eventually spreading across the plains during the late afternoon and evening period. Light snow spread across the far eastern plains overnight, and tapered off the morning the 9th.  In the Front Range Front Range mountains, foothills, and South Park storm totals ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  The early season snow brought a welcome but relatively brief period of relief for the ongoing wildfires.  Along the urban corridor, storm totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches mainly west of Interstate 25 and over the Palmer Divide south and southeast of Denver.  The heavy and wet snow downed some fully leaved trees and snapped large  tree limbs in Boulder and Golden, and caused electrical outages.  Boulder was hardest hit with 6500 outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included:  10-12 inches over the Cameron wildfire southwest of Red Feather Lakes, 10.5 inches in Rist Canyon, 9 inches at Eldorado Springs, 8 inches, 7 miles southwest of Virginia Dale,  7 inches near Jamestown and Red Feather Lakes;  6.5 inches near Estes Park and Horsetooth Mountain; 6 inches near Allenspark and 5.5 inches near Ward.

CZ_NAME: LEADVILLE VICINITY / LAKE COUNTY BELOW 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system impacted southern Colorado from September 8th through the 10th. This system brought an end to the above normal warmth, and provided the region with winter like weather. Strong northerly winds, heavy snow across the mountains and mountain valleys, and a wintry mix of rain, snow, sleet, and freezing rain across parts of the I-25 corridor were all observed during this time. Additionally, well below normal temperatures brought an end to the growing season over much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total of 15.5 inches of snow. 6 NNE Turquoise Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete and clustered thunderstorms formed along and behind a SE-NW oriented boundary associated with an upper level disturbance moving east across eastern Colorado. As the boundary moved closer to the CO-KS border, the storms merged into a linear system extending from Kit Carson County north into Wyoming. To the south in Cheyenne County, a separate cluster of storms extended across the state line into Greeley County, KS. The storms produced up to ping pong sized hail in Cheyenne County and a 61 mph wind gust in Kit Carson County.

A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.

A strong storm system moved across the area the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  This system produced severe thunderstorms and high winds, across northeast Colorado, with snow across the mountains, valley areas and higher foothills.  In addition rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred across the plains.   Storm totals included:  15 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 7 miles northwest of Red Feather Lakes; 11 inches near Cameron Pass; with 4 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Strong northwest winds developed across the northeast plains on the evening of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th, with peak gusts from 58 to 71 mph.  ||Severe thunderstorms  on the evening of the 8th, produced powerful and destructive wind gusts along with nickel to quarter size hail, over portions of northeast Colorado.  Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron.  The swath of damaging winds extended from central Washington County into Logan County and across western areas of Phillips and Sedgwick counties.  Widespread crop and property damage was observed.

A stationary thunderstorm producing prolonged heavy rainfall over Dundy County Nebraska began to develop west into Yuma County during the night.  The heavy rainfall continued into the overnight hours before ending.  Up to six inches of rainfall occurred, and was centered over Highway 34, with amounts declining a few miles west of Wray.  The axis of heavy rainfall was also centered over the Republican River Basin, which caused flooding in Wray well after the storm had ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areal pictures show the flooding mainly confined to the Republican River valley continuing well into the daylight hours.  Fields were flooded along the river, as well as water running over roads.  Anything in Wray along the Republican River was flooded.  This included parks, buildings, and some streets that didn't have a large capacity for drainage underneath.  Based on footage during the daytime, Highway 34 west out of Wray did not have water over it.  Damage from the flooding is not know at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete and clustered thunderstorms formed along and behind a SE-NW oriented boundary associated with an upper level disturbance moving east across eastern Colorado. As the boundary moved closer to the CO-KS border, the storms merged into a linear system extending from Kit Carson County north into Wyoming. To the south in Cheyenne County, a separate cluster of storms extended across the state line into Greeley County, KS. The storms produced up to ping pong sized hail in Cheyenne County and a 61 mph wind gust in Kit Carson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball sized hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought snow and blowing snow to the northern mountains, with strong post frontal bora winds to the Front Range Foothills, I-25 corridor and northeast plains.  Peak wind gusts from 58 to 85 mph were recorded across the region.  A few trees were reportedly knocked down as well.   In addition to the strong winds, storm totals in the mountains ranged from 3 to 9 inches.  ||In and near the Front Range mountains and foothills, the more impressive gusts included:  91 mph near Loveland Pass; 85 mph 3 miles south of Boulder, 80 mph, 3 miles northwest of Boulder; 79 mph near Marshall, 78 mph near Glen Haven and 75 mph near Rocky Flats.  Across the northeast plains of Colorado, the stronger gusts included:  76 mph in Fort Morgan, 70 mph at Cedar Point; 69 mph at Akron; 68 mph, 11 miles northwest of Westplains, and 67 mph near Crook and 8 miles south of Holyoke.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included 69 mph at Akron-Washington County Airport.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low brought an end to record heat through the first week of September and provided Denver with one of its earliest measurable snowfalls on record. By the evening of September 7th, a series of cold fronts progressed southward from Wyoming into Colorado, and dropped the temperature down into the low 30s by early morning of the 8th. Snow developed across the Front Range mountains and foothills, while a mix of rain and snow developed along the I-25 corridor.  On the 8th, accumulating snow was mostly confined to the higher elevations, eventually spreading across the plains during the late afternoon and evening period. Light snow spread across the far eastern plains overnight, and tapered off the morning the 9th.  In the Front Range Front Range mountains, foothills, and South Park storm totals ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  The early season snow brought a welcome but relatively brief period of relief for the ongoing wildfires.  Along the urban corridor, storm totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches mainly west of Interstate 25 and over the Palmer Divide south and southeast of Denver.  The heavy and wet snow downed some fully leaved trees and snapped large  tree limbs in Boulder and Golden, and caused electrical outages.  Boulder was hardest hit with 6500 outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals included 4 to 5 inches in west Fort Collins, with 2 to 4 inches over the rest of Fort Collins, Laporte and Loveland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed along the dry line in central Colorado increasing in coverage as they moved east. The cells merged into clusters along a line as they entered the area producing quarter sized hail and up to 64 mph wind gusts in eastern Colorado.
A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 18 miles north of Grand Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Gulch Fire began at the end of July due to lightning 18 miles north of Grand Junction and grew to 139,007 acres before it was contained in mid September. The combination of drought-stressed vegetation, unseasonably hot weather and steep terrain led to weeks of active burning. Smoke columns were often visible from Grand Junction and the surrounding area as the wildfire exhibited extreme fire behavior. During the night of August 18, the fire grew quickly due to thunderstorm winds up to 40 mph for a three to four hour period. As a result, the fire increased by more than 30,000 acres that night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of an upper level trough moving northwest across the state, coupled with a strong surface pressure gradient, led to numerous reports of high wind gusts across  the region.  Most of the wind damage occurred with the passage of a rare derecho; a large fast-moving complex of thunderstorms with powerful straight-line winds.  In addition, strong wind gusts from 59 to 71 mph not associated with the derecho, occurred prior to and following the passage of this system over the urban corridor and plains of northeast Colorado.||By midday the derecho, which originated over the Great Basin, moved over the northern and central mountains, and then rapidly northeast across the I-25 corridor by mid afternoon.  These storms then tracked across the plains from late afternoon into the early evening hours.  Wind gusts from 60 to 80 mph were common with a few gusts exceeding 90 mph.  The highest gust observed was 110 mph, at the Winter Park Ski area, around 12000 feet.  Xcel Energy said in a press release that more than 208,000 customers lost power because of the storm statewide. The intense wind toppled a billboard that crashed onto Denver bakery.  Two delivery trucks parked nearby were heavily damaged.  Two injuries occurred in Denver, one seriously.  A man and his nine-year-old daughter were injured by a fallen tree.  They were standing in front of a family member���s house when the incident occurred.  The tree impaled the father through his back and he required several surgeries.  His daughter sustained cuts and bruises that required stitches.

Temperatures were above normal and precipitation was below normal across much of Colorado during the month of August. As a result, drought conditions in southwest and west-central Colorado either persisted or worsened with the drought expanding north to encompass much of the western slope by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought persisted across the eastern and central portions of the zone and expanded to include the entirety of the forecast zone by the end of August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved across the area the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  This system produced severe thunderstorms and high winds, across northeast Colorado, with snow across the mountains, valley areas and higher foothills.  In addition rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches occurred across the plains.   Storm totals included:  15 inches, 9 miles south-southeast of Gould and 7 miles northwest of Red Feather Lakes; 11 inches near Cameron Pass; with 4 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Strong northwest winds developed across the northeast plains on the evening of the 8th into the early morning hours of the 9th, with peak gusts from 58 to 71 mph.  ||Severe thunderstorms  on the evening of the 8th, produced powerful and destructive wind gusts along with nickel to quarter size hail, over portions of northeast Colorado.  Peak wind gusts up to 80 mph occurred, with localized gusts from 100 to 113 mph, in and near Akron.  The swath of damaging winds extended from central Washington County into Logan County and across western areas of Phillips and Sedgwick counties.  Widespread crop and property damage was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included 64 mph, 5 miles north of Pawnee Pass.

Across the northeast plains of Colorado, the stronger gusts included:  76 mph in Fort Morgan, 70 mph at Cedar Point; 69 mph at Akron; 68 mph, 11 miles northwest of Westplains, and 67 mph near Crook and 8 miles south of Holyoke.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included 73 mph, 6 miles southwest of Carr; 65 mph, 2 miles north-northeast of Natural Fort; 61 mph, 7 miles north-northeast of Purcell and 7 miles north of Rockport.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low brought an end to record heat through the first week of September and provided Denver with one of its earliest measurable snowfalls on record. By the evening of September 7th, a series of cold fronts progressed southward from Wyoming into Colorado, and dropped the temperature down into the low 30s by early morning of the 8th. Snow developed across the Front Range mountains and foothills, while a mix of rain and snow developed along the I-25 corridor.  On the 8th, accumulating snow was mostly confined to the higher elevations, eventually spreading across the plains during the late afternoon and evening period. Light snow spread across the far eastern plains overnight, and tapered off the morning the 9th.  In the Front Range Front Range mountains, foothills, and South Park storm totals ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  The early season snow brought a welcome but relatively brief period of relief for the ongoing wildfires.  Along the urban corridor, storm totals ranged from 3 to 6 inches mainly west of Interstate 25 and over the Palmer Divide south and southeast of Denver.  The heavy and wet snow downed some fully leaved trees and snapped large  tree limbs in Boulder and Golden, and caused electrical outages.  Boulder was hardest hit with 6500 outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals  across the Denver area ranged from 1 to  3 inches.  At Denver International Airport, 1.0 inches was the official measurement on the 8th, making it the second earliest measurable snowfall for Denver to date.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A worsening drought that started in the spring of 2020, continued through September. Outside of an early season snow on the 8th, the month of September was another unseasonably warm and dry period. The combination of hot and mostly dry conditions, in addition to critically dry fuels from years of beetle kill, resulted in a continuation and rapid expansion of the wildfire.   The Cameron Peak fire, the largest in the state's history started on August 13th, and consumed an additional 102,075 acres of forestland through the month of September.  It continued to burn through October, and consumed a total of 208,663 acres of forestland by late October. A total of 441 buildings were destroyed, including: 213 residences, 211 outbuildings, and 17 buildings associated with Shambala Mountain Center. It resulted in extensive voluntary and mandatory evacuations and numerous road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the month of September, the Cameron Peak wildfire consumed an additional 102,075  acres of forestland.  Very cold temperatures after an early season snow on the 8th settled the fire down temporarily, but then a return to very warm and dry weather, coupled with gusty downslope winds to 50 mph on the 26th, fanned the flames and allowed the Cameron Peak fire to continue its expansion through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of December with extreme (D3) conditions across the northwest portion of the zone and the exceptional (D4) category elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of early season winter storms was followed by a period of dry weather beginning on November 23. The dry pattern resulted in warm days, cold nights and light winds. The weather pattern changed on December 10 as a series of Pacific storms passed through the area. This brought new snowfall amounts from 15 to 20 inches across the area. A small group of backcountry skiers unintentionally triggered a soft slab avalanche. This would be the second fatal avalanche in two days in Western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A soft slab avalanche was triggered on the north face of Battleship, southeast of Ophir, Colorado by a group of backcountry skiers after a week long period of light to moderate snowfall. The avalanche was small relative to the path and destructive enough to injure, bury or kill a person. The avalanche reached depths from 12 to 20 inches, was about 700 feet wide and ran 1600 vertical feet, from the alpine into a below treeline creek bed. The Colorado Avalanche Information Center has rated the avalanche danger as Considerable (Level 3) above treeline and Moderate (Level 2) below treeline. Three skiers were either caught or partially caught by the avalanche with only one survivor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front progressed into western Colorado and brought moderate to heavy snow over portions of the central and southern mountains. A secondary piece of energy lifted over the area during the latter half of the event which brought additional moderate snowfall to the region. A tightened gradient out ahead of and with the arrival of the front produced wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH at times which led to hazardous travel conditions in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the area with up to 35 inches estimated at the Spud Mountain SNOTEL site. Winds gusted over 60 MPH at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough transitioned to a closed low as it approached the Four Corners Region. Unsettled southwesterly flow ahead of this system led to moderate to heavy snow over the southern valleys and mountains before the low lifted northeast across western Colorado. This transition led to more widespread precipitation with accumulating snow occurring in most locations, particularly under heavier snow bands. However, there were some breaks in precipitation as drier air worked into the area at times. Wind gusts in the valley exceeded 30 mph during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts of up to 18 inches reported.

CZ_NAME: N & NE ELBERT COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / N LINCOLN COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong bora winds developed following the passage of a vigorous cold front that swept across the urban corridor and northeast plains.  In addition to the strong winds, brief bursts of heavy snow with rates from 1 to 2 inches in less than one hour, developed shortly after the passage of the cold front.  Consequently, road conditions quickly deteriorated in the Front Range mountains, foothills and I-25 corridor from around Fort Collins northward.  Peak wind gust reports included:  69 mph at Akron and Grover; 64 mph near Briggsdale and Fort Morgan; 63 mph at Limon; 62 mph along US 85 near the Colorado/Wyoming border; 61 mph in Haxtun and Sterling; and 60 mph at Denver International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 mph was measured at Limon Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front progressed into western Colorado and brought moderate to heavy snow over portions of the central and southern mountains. A secondary piece of energy lifted over the area during the latter half of the event which brought additional moderate snowfall to the region. A tightened gradient out ahead of and with the arrival of the front produced wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH at times which led to hazardous travel conditions in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific trough transitioned to a closed low as it approached the Four Corners Region. Unsettled southwesterly flow ahead of this system led to moderate to heavy snow over the southern valleys and mountains before the low lifted northeast across western Colorado. This transition led to more widespread precipitation with accumulating snow occurring in most locations, particularly under heavier snow bands. However, there were some breaks in precipitation as drier air worked into the area at times. Wind gusts in the valley exceeded 30 mph during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 3 miles south-southwest of Yellow Jacket.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yellow Jacket Fire began at the end of June due to lightning 3 miles south-southwest of Yellow Jacket near Canyons of the Ancients Monument and grew to 395 acres before it was contained in early July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Midlevel moisture gradually increased across western Colorado as a ridge of high pressure remained overhead. This increase in moisture was enough to fuel scattered showers and thunderstorms on Sunday, July 12th and again on Monday, July 13th. However, the lower levels of the atmosphere remained dry which led to minimal rainfall and gusty and erratic outflow winds from showers and storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet rounding the base of a deep area of low pressure and associated cold front led to strong southwest flow and breezy conditions across western Colorado. Some wind gusts met High Wind Warning criteria across northwest and west-central Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though average temperatures were below normal across much of western Colorado during the month of December, precipitation was still below normal. As a result, drought conditions persisted across the entirety of the western slope and remained unchanged from what was experienced in November through the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The status of the drought remained unchanged through the month of December with exceptional (D4) conditions across all but the far southwest portion of the zone where the extreme (D3) drought continued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of early season winter storms was followed by a period of dry weather beginning on November 23. The dry pattern resulted in warm days, cold nights and light winds. The weather pattern changed on December 10 as a series of Pacific storms passed through the area. This brought new snowfall amounts from 15 to 20 inches across the area. A small group of backcountry skiers unintentionally triggered a soft slab avalanche.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A soft slab avalanche was triggered near Ohio Pass by a group of backcountry skiers after a week long period of light to moderate snowfall. The avalanche was small relative to the path and destructive enough to injure, bury or kill a person. The avalanche reached depths from 3 to 7 feet, was 400 foot wide and the debris field rain for about 400 feet. The Colorado Avalanche Information Center has rated the avalanche danger as Considerable (Level 3) above treeline and Moderate (Level 2) below treeline. One skier was caught in the avalanche, buried and did not survive.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backcounty skier was caught buried and killed in an avalanche in the First Creek Drainage, of Berthoud Pass.  The avalanche occurred in an area locally known as Chimney Chute, which is a steep, narrow, north-facing below treeline chute. The victim was located by Grand County Search and Rescue team members with an avalanche probe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A backcounty skier was caught buried and killed in an avalanche in the First Creek Drainage, of Berthoud Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and associated cold front dropped into the region from the Northern Rockies. This cold front brought a strong band of showers to areas north of I-70 with marginal instability fueling snow squalls which led to hazardous travel. By the time this system exited the area it had produced moderate snow over portions of the northwest and west-central Colorado mountains. Winds gusted from 60 to 75 mph across the higher peaks at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 15 inches estimated at the Tower SNOTEL site. Winds gusted from 40 to 60 mph as robust snow squalls rolled through the area between 1700 and 1900 MST on the evening of the 22nd.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and associated cold front dropped into the region from the Northern Rockies. This cold front brought a strong band of showers to areas north of I-70 with marginal instability fueling snow squalls which led to hazardous travel. By the time this system exited the area it had produced moderate snow over portions of the northwest and west-central Colorado mountains. Winds gusted from 60 to 75 mph across the higher peaks at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted from 50 to 74 mph as robust snow squalls rolled through the area between 1800 and 2000 MST on the evening of the 22nd. A trained spotter recorded visibility of less than 1/4 mile for 30 minutes and a peak wind gust of 49 MPH with a squall northwest of Avon. These snow squalls led to white-out conditions and resulted in the closure of Interstate 70 for a short period. At least 10 to 20 wrecks and slide offs were reported along the I-70 corridor between Gypsum, Wolcott and Vail Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grizzly Creek Fire began in mid August from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs. It grew to a total of 32,631 acres before it was contained in mid December. As a result of the fire, major Interstate-70 was closed through Glenwood Canyon for more than a week which greatly impacted summer travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure and associated cold front trekked across western Colorado and produced moderate to heavy snowfall over the southwest and west-central mountains. Favorable southwesterly flow ahead of this system kept snow levels high before colder air brought levels down to valley floors during the latter half of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 16 inches.

In addition to the strong winds, brief bursts of heavy snow with rates from 1 to 2 inches in less than one hour, developed shortly after the passage of the cold front.  Consequently, road conditions quickly deteriorated in the Front Range mountains, foothills and I-25 corridor from around Fort Collins northward.  Peak wind gust reports included:  69 mph at Akron and Grover; 64 mph near Briggsdale and Fort Morgan; 63 mph at Limon; 62 mph along US 85 near the Colorado/Wyoming border; 61 mph in Haxtun and Sterling; and 60 mph at Denver International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 61 mph was measured in Haxtun.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light west-northwesterly flow remained over western Colorado as high pressure began to amplify over the Desert Southwest. Strong surface heating resulted in marginal instability and, when paired with residual monsoonal moisture, isolated thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain. As these cells drifted into the valleys they began to collapse and produce severe thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Blake Field Airport in Delta.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sand Creek Fire began in mid June due to lightning 20 miles northwest of Pagosa Springs. The fire grew to 107 acres before it was finally contained in early August after receiving monsoonal moisture in late July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 1 mile east of the Utah/Colorado border between Brown's Park and Dinosaur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pots Fire began at the end of July due to lightning 1 mile east of the Utah/Colorado border between Brown's Park and Dinosaur. The fire grew to 235 acres before it was contained in early August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire 18 miles north of Grand Junction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pine Gulch Fire began at the end of July due to lightning 18 miles north of Grand Junction and grew to 139,007 acres before it was contained in mid September. The combination of drought-stressed vegetation, unseasonably hot weather and steep terrain led to weeks of active burning. Smoke columns were often visible from Grand Junction and the surrounding area as the wildfire exhibited extreme fire behavior. During the night of August 18, the fire grew quickly due to thunderstorm winds up to 40 mph for a three to four hour period. As a result, the fire increased by more than 30,000 acres that night. As of August 27, 2020 the Pine Gulch Fire became the largest wildfire in Colorado State history, surpassing the Hayman Fire that burned near Colorado Springs in the summer of 2002.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grizzly Creek Fire began in mid August from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs. It grew to a total of 32,631 acres before it was contained in mid December. As a result of the fire, major Interstate-70 was closed through Glenwood Canyon for more than a week which greatly impacted summer travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the month of October, the Cameron Peak wildfire more than doubled its size. The first half of October could be characterized as unseasonably warm, dry and windy.  These conditions allowed the fire to explode from 102,075 acres on the 1st, to 208,663 acres by the 31st.  Gusty westerly winds associated the passing weather disturbances exacerbated the growth of the fire, and made it the largest wildfire to date in Colorado history.  A total of 444 buildings were destroyed, including: 224 residences, 220 outbuildings, and 17 buildings associated with Shambala Mountain Center. It resulted in extensive voluntary and mandatory evacuations and numerous road closures.  The cost to fight the Cameron Peak fire was estimated to be $134 million.  The preliminary property damage estimate of $91.8 million only includes the residences and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, dry conditions and warm temperatures through most of October, preceding a period of heavy snow from October 23-25th, caused the Cameron Peak wildfire to double in size.  The growth of the fire was minimized significantly after the snow storm, although it continued to burn through November.  Initial damage estimate of $91.8 million is based off the average price of $400,000 per residence in Larimer County, and $10,000 per outbuilding.  The cost to fight the wildfire was estimated at $134 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds on the 21st pushed the East Troublesome Fire across the Continental Divide. All roads into Estes Park were closed. Mandatory evacuation orders were issued for the western part of Rocky Mountain National Park and for the west side of Estes Park. A storm system in late October brought heavy snow and much colder temperatures; it  gave firefighters the break they needed to start containment of the fire, cancel the evacuations and start assessing the damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire crossed State the Continental Divide on the 22nd, and reached the western edge of Estes Park on the 23rd.

This increase in moisture was enough to fuel scattered showers and thunderstorms on Sunday, July 12th and again on Monday, July 13th. However, the lower levels of the atmosphere remained dry which led to minimal rainfall and gusty and erratic outflow winds from showers and storms.

The East Troublesome Fire started on the afternoon of October 14th and continued through the end of the month; iIt became the second largest wildfire in Colorado to date, and one of the most destructive.  The origination point was northeast of Kremmling in Grand County on the Arapaho National Forest. The fire was fueled by widespread drought, numerous dead and down beetle-killed trees, red flag weather conditions created by high winds and dry conditions, along with poor humidity recovery overnight. The combination of these factors led to unprecedented, wind-driven, active fire behavior with rapid spread during the overnight hours.  The peak fire spread of 87,093 acres occurred between late afternoon on the 21st and the early afternoon of the 22nd. The size of the fire exploded from 18,550 acres to 187,964 acres during this 24-hr period. The fire crossed State Highway 125 on the afternoon of the 21st and spread eastward into the Rocky Mountain National Park on the 22nd, crossing the Continental Divide and reaching the western edge of Estes Park on October 23rd.  ||The East Troublesome Fire burned 193,774 acres through the end of October, which was nearly 15 percent of the land in Grand County. Several mandatory evacuations were issued, impacting more than 35,000 people.  An elderly couple died on October 21st when they refused to evacuate and took shelter in the basement of their home.  The East Troublesome Fire destroyed 555 structures.  Nine buildings suffered major damage and 34 sustained minor damage.  Among the buildings destroyed, 366 were residential and 189 were outbuildings. More than 200 were primary residences.   Many ranches reported a significant or total loss of hay intended for winter feeding of livestock. Damages and impacts associated with agricultural land and operations were ongoing.  The burned lands incorporated significant grazing leases held by local producers, and impacts to agriculture irrigation supply and delivery were still being assessed.  A winter storm in late October brought very cold temperatures, a blanket of heavy wet snow and much lighter winds.  These factors resulted in a dramatic drop in fire behavior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Troublesome Fire destroyed 555 structures.  Two fatalities were confirmed.  Nine buildings suffered major damage and 34 sustained minor damage.  Among the buildings destroyed, 366 were residential and 189 were outbuildings. Ten buildings belonging to businesses were destroyed in the fire.  Grand County estimated that debris removal could exceed over 30,000 cubic yards and cost more than $27 million to haul off.  The property damage estimate only includes the destroyed residences and outbuildings.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving wildfire developed in the foothills of Boulder County.  The Cal-Wood Fire started near Jamestown and quickly spread eastward through the foothills of Boulder County.  The fire occurred during a protracted period of hot and dry weather, and a drought that started in the Spring and continued into Fall.  It was pushed by strong winds. The National Center for Atmospheric Research���s Mesa lab recorded gusts up to 59 mph on the 17th.  The fire quickly consumed nearly 8,788 acres within a 24-hr period.  At one point, more than 1,600 residences and nearly 3,000 people were under evacuation orders, including the small town of Jamestown.  By the end of the month, the Cal-Wood fire had consumed 10,105 acres, and became the largest wildfire in Boulder County history, surpassing Fourmile Fire in 2010. The Cal-Wood fire burned a total of 26 homes, with preliminary property damage estimates of $36.9 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire quickly consumed a total 10,105 acres and burned 26 homes.  It became the largest wildfire in Boulder County history.  According to real estate value website Zillow, the 26 homes and properties damaged or destroyed by the CalWood fire had a collective value of approximately $36.9 million.  The CalWood properties that were destroyed held an average value of $1.7 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several weeks of warm, windy and dry weather that fueled the two largest wildfires in the state's history; a powerful storm system brought welcome relief as it produced heavy snow and frigid temperatures to northern Colorado. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals from 4 to 6 inches were observed.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving storm system moved across northern Colorado and produced a period of very strong winds the mountains and foothills initially.  The strong winds spread across the northeast plains behind the passage of a strong cold front.  In spite of the cooler temperatures, fire weather conditions remained extreme in the Cameron Peak fire due to strong winds and low relative humidity.||In and near the Front Range mountains and foothills, peak gusts included:  82 mph near Buckeye, 81 mph at the junction of CO93 and CO72; 77 mph atop Berthoud Pass and 68 mph near LaPorte.  Across the urban corridor and northeast plains, peak gusts included:  71 mph near Briggsdale, 69 mph near Limon, 68 mph at Akron, 65 mph near Wiggins, 64 mph near Eaton, Pawnee Buttes, Platteville and Severence; 63 mph at Cedar Point, near Natural Fort Rest Area and Nunn; 62 mph at Superior, 60 mph at Platteville, near Sterling and 11 miles west-northwest of Westplains; 59 mph at Denver International Airport, 7 miles south-southwest of Hugo, near Loveland, and 7 miles northeast of Purcell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included:  82 mph, 3 miles east-southeast of Buckeye; 68 mph, 6 miles southwest of Carr and 2 miles south-southwest of Laporte; and 63 mph, 2 miles north-northeast of Natural Fort Rest Area and Nunn; with 59 mph, 3 miles west-southwest of Loveland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight lows ranged from 20 to 27 degrees with a low temperature of 27 degrees recorded at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several weeks of warm, windy and dry weather that fueled the two largest wildfires in the state's history; a powerful storm system brought welcome relief as it produced heavy snow and frigid temperatures to northern Colorado.  In the Front Range mountains and foothills, storm totals ranged from 10 to 20 inches.  A trained spotter near Virginia Dale was the big winner with 28.3 inches of snow.  Along the urban corridor, storm totals from 4 to 12 inches were observed, with the heaviest amounts along and generally west of I-25 and over Weld County, where localized bands of heavy snow developed.||Some of the more impressive storm totals included:  28.3 inches, 7 miles west-southwest of Virginia Dale; 19.5 inches, 10 miles west-northwest of Livermore; 18.3 inches near Red Feather Lakes; 18 inches near Horsetooth Mountain; 17.7 inches, 3 miles west of Fort Collins; 15 inches, 7 miles west of Rustic; 14.3 inches near Allenspark; 13.3 inches near Wellington; 12.9 inches in southeast Boulder and Nederland; 12.8 inches near Loveland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals from 4 to 9 inches were observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Midlevel moisture gradually increased across western Colorado as a ridge of high pressure remained overhead. This increase in moisture was enough to fuel scattered showers and thunderstorms on Sunday, July 12th and again on Monday, July 13th. However, the lower levels of the atmosphere remained dry which led to minimal rainfall and gusty and erratic outflow winds from showers and storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Eagle County Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light west-northwesterly flow remained over western Colorado as high pressure began to amplify over the Desert Southwest. Strong surface heating resulted in marginal instability and, when paired with residual monsoonal moisture, isolated thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain. As these cells drifted into the valleys they began to collapse and produce severe thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 62 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport.

Light west-northwesterly flow remained over western Colorado as high pressure began to amplify over the Desert Southwest. Strong surface heating resulted in marginal instability and, when paired with residual monsoonal moisture, isolated thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain. CZ_NAME: COLORADO SPRINGS VICINITY / SOUTHERN EL PASO COUNTY / RAMPART RANGE BELOW 7500 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front surged south across the area during the late evening hours, with strong northerly winds accompanying this frontal passage. Wind gusts to around 60 mph were produced along and to the east of the I-25 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated Weather Observing System KABH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough moved through western Colorado on the morning and afternoon of Saturday, June 6th, causing a widespread severe weather event that would become the first derecho to impact the Western Slope in well over a decade. Severe storms produced wind gusts upwards of 60 to 80 mph on average with some gusts topping 80 to 100 mph in some areas due to strong downburst winds. There were multiple reports of uprooted trees, downed power lines, at least one roof was torn off with others damaged, and several houses/cabins and vehicles were damaged by falling trees and/or large hail. Very heavy rain also fell which led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the Dragon Road automated weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough moved through western Colorado on the morning and afternoon of Saturday, June 6th, causing a widespread severe weather event that would become the first derecho to impact the Western Slope in well over a decade. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary shortwave trough lifted across western Colorado and produced scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms. In addition to brief heavy rain, gusty outflow winds were common with storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was measured by the automated weather station at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and strong Pacific storm approached the Great Basin on Friday, June 5th. Hot, dry and windy conditions preceded this moisture which led to critical fire weather conditions across a large portion of the region Friday afternoon. Grand Junction, Colorado also reached a record high of 100 degrees that day. Thunderstorms formed on the nose of the arriving moisture late in the afternoon on the 5th across the Four Corners region. A strong, northward moving outflow boundary was produced from these initial storms and survived to the Wyoming border by late in the evening. The fast movement of the boundary enhanced the wind threat from thunderstorms forming along it which led to numerous reports of strong outflow winds and localized damage. In addition, lightning and strong winds exacerbated the growth of existing fires and contributed to numerous fire starts across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 60 mph was measured by an automated weather station west-southwest of Hiawatha.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The exceptional (D4) category persisted across all but the far southwest portion of the zone through the end of November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough moved through western Colorado on the morning and afternoon of Saturday, June 6th, causing a widespread severe weather event that would become the first derecho to impact the Western Slope in well over a decade. Severe storms produced wind gusts upwards of 60 to 80 mph on average with some gusts topping 80 to 100 mph in some areas due to strong downburst winds. There were multiple reports of uprooted trees, downed power lines, at least one roof was torn off with others damaged, and several houses/cabins and vehicles were damaged by falling trees and/or large hail. Very heavy rain also fell which led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris left over from the 2019 avalanches became dislodged by heavy rain in the Marshall Creek Basin. A large amount of trees, wood, and other debris from old mining structures travelled down the drainage until it reached the culvert near CR K-69, near the Idarado mine. The water and debris overwhelmed the drainage and began flowing across the roadway. The downstream portion of the roadway began to scour with water and eroded the embankment along the southern shoulder. The road was closed for a few hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and strong Pacific storm approached the Great Basin on Friday, June 5th. Hot, dry and windy conditions preceded this moisture which led to critical fire weather conditions across a large portion of the region Friday afternoon. Grand Junction, Colorado also reached a record high of 100 degrees that day. Thunderstorms formed on the nose of the arriving moisture late in the afternoon on the 5th across the Four Corners region. A strong, northward moving outflow boundary was produced from these initial storms and survived to the Wyoming border by late in the evening. The fast movement of the boundary enhanced the wind threat from thunderstorms forming along it which led to numerous reports of strong outflow winds and localized damage. In addition, lightning and strong winds exacerbated the growth of existing fires and contributed to numerous fire starts across western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported a downed power line across the road in Orchard Mesa, caused by wind gusts estimated to be around 64 mph. Additionally, a trained spotter nearby had a moderate to large tree uprooted and blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65-year-old woman was killed and her friend was injured when struck by lightning while walking in the 200 block of Silver Queen South in the Edgemont subdivision. The lightning bolt first struck a tree and moved laterally to strike the woman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough moved through western Colorado on the morning and afternoon of Saturday, June 6th, causing a widespread severe weather event that would become the first derecho to impact the Western Slope in well over a decade. Severe storms produced wind gusts upwards of 60 to 80 mph on average with some gusts topping 80 to 100 mph in some areas due to strong downburst winds. There were multiple reports of uprooted trees, downed power lines, at least one roof was torn off with others damaged, and several houses/cabins and vehicles were damaged by falling trees and/or large hail. Very heavy rain also fell which led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes on Dunn Drive in Craig, CO had severe damage. One home suffered damage to three sections of metal roofing. Also, the SW corner of a large pole barn lost many roof panels. Another house to the west lost 50% of its roof.  A separate 2 car garage at the residence also lost the SW corner of the roof. Another home had part of its attached garage roof blown off when the south facing garage doors were blown in.  A shed was also destroyed on this property. Another home, just to the south, also lost its roof. Finally, another home on the same road almost lost the entire metal roof off their single wide trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire began from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grizzly Creek Fire began in mid August from human causes 1 mile east of Glenwood Springs. It grew to a total of 32,631 acres before it was contained in mid December. As a result of the fire, major Interstate-70 was closed through Glenwood Canyon for more than a week which greatly impacted summer travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level system crossed the Rockies on this day, bringing very strong winds to all of the higher terrain and parts of the I-25 corridor. This system also brought a quick blast of snow to the mountains. The higher wind gusts and this snow helped provide a setup for snow squalls, which were observed over parts of Chaffee county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture paired with the support of a passing shortwave aloft produced numerous showers and thunderstorms across western Colorado. Some stronger storms produced heavy rain in a short period of time which led to localized flash flooding in Garfield County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture continued to advect into western Colorado on the backside of an area of high pressure. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over the area on July 27th, many of which produced periods of moderate to heavy rain. After receiving 1 to 3 inches of rain on average over the course of a few days, portions of southwest Colorado were well saturated and vulnerable to flash flooding and debris flows. A couple of mudslides were reported across San Miguel and Ouray counties as a result of the heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide closed County Road 361, to Yankee Boy Basin, near Twin Falls as a result of heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture continued to advect into western Colorado on the backside of an area of high pressure. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over the area on July 27th, many of which produced periods of moderate to heavy rain. After receiving 1 to 3 inches of rain on average over the course of a few days, portions of southwest Colorado were well saturated and vulnerable to flash flooding and debris flows. A couple of mudslides were reported across San Miguel and Ouray counties as a result of the heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide that measured 35 feet wide and was 3 to 5 feet deep occurred around midday and blocked Bridal Veil Road. More than 40 people were temporarily stranded while county road crews created a temporary passage. Additionally, boulders the size of small refrigerators were seen tumbling down. Radar estimates showed one hour rain rates of up to half an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The east-central portion of Colorado saw non-thunderstorm high sustained winds and gusts associated with a cold front beginning in the afternoon hours of December 22nd that continued until the afternoon hours of December 23rd. The highest sustained winds were recorded at 48 mph 17 miles north of Flagler, CO with the highest wind gust recorded by the Burlington, CO ASOS at 75 mph. These winds caused some damage in the area along with reduced visibilities due to blowing dust and blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Behind a cold front, high winds up to 48 mph sustained and wind gusts up to 75 mph were observed in Kit Carson County from the afternoon of 12/22/20 through the morning of 12/23/20. The max sustained winds of 48 mph were recorded at 17 miles north of Flagler, CO and the max wind gust of 75 mph was recorded at the Burlington, CO ASOS. Key impacts: high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The east-central portion of Colorado saw non-thunderstorm high sustained winds and gusts associated with a cold front beginning in the afternoon hours of December 22nd that continued until the afternoon hours of December 23rd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing snow caused by high winds was reported via social media 2 miles west of Wray, CO on the morning of 12/23/20. The blowing snow reduced visibility down to less than 1/4 of a mile. Key impacts: hazardous traveling conditions, blowing snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Behind a cold front, high winds up to 45 mph sustained were observed in Yuma County from the night of 12/22/20 through the morning of 12/23/20. The max sustained winds of 45 mph were recorded at the Yuma, CO AWOS. The mesonet site near Idalia measured a gust of 62 MPH. The winds broke off a 5-inch diameter tree limb in Joes that was 10 feet long which landed on house during the event. The winds also caused blowing snow which reduced visibility down to less than 1/4 of a mile on the morning of 12/23/20. Key impacts: blowing debris, blowing snow, high winds.

Seven people were injured with four in serious condition in this accident. Key impacts: hazardous traveling conditions, vehicle accidents, blowing dust, dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of December 27th, two large bands of snow developed and impacted east-central Colorado. The northern most band formed over northeast Colorado and gradually moved south across Yuma County into northern Kit Carson County. The second band formed over southeast Colorado and slowly moved north into Cheyenne County and southern Kit Carson County before moving east. Throughout the multi-day event, numerous small bands developed and moved across east-central Colorado producing snow that ranged in depth from a trace to 4.1 inches, with amounts increasing from south to north across east-central Colorado. The highest snowfall totals occurred in northern Yuma County. While snow amounts were low, one passenger vehicle accident occurred on I-70 in Kit Carson County and multiple slide-offs were reported in both Kit Carson and Yuma counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow produced up to 2 inches of snow. The snow accumulated on roads, creating slick conditions that caused several vehicle slide-offs on I-70 and Highway 24. One non-injury accident involving a passenger vehicle was reported at 4:21 PM MST on December 28th located at mile marker 448 on I-70. Key impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong downslope winds developed along the Front Range.  Peak wind gusts reached 95 mph near Caribou and Virginia Dale, with a gust of 93 mph near Berthoud Pass.  Peak gusts elsewhere included:  87 mph near Tolland; 83 mph near White Ranch Open Space; 82 mph near Rocky Flats; 81 mph near Nederland; 80 mph near Georgetown; 78 mph near Downieville and Ward; 77 mph near Crisman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gusts included:  83 mph at White Ranch Open Space; 82 mph at the Junction of state highways 72 and 93; 80 mph, 1 mile north of Georgetown; 78 mph at Downieville; 76 mph at the National Wind Technology Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon several weak landspout tornadoes were reported from slow moving storms in northern Cheyenne County.  No damage was reported from these landspouts.||After the landspouts had ended, a weak front moving east across East Central Colorado caused a brief dust storm to occur south of Burlington near a feedlot.||During the late afternoon another round of thunderstorms developed west into Colorado from Kansas.  A gust of 59 MPH was reported from one of these storms in Hale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Third brief landspout tornado observed by NWS employee and Viaero webcam. This one began as the second landspout tornado of the day was ending.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon a cluster of weak thunderstorms moved into Cheyenne County from the south.  Winds coming out from the storms turned an irrigation pivot on its side southwest of Arapahoe.  Peak wind gust reports for these storms was measured at 70 MPH near Arapahoe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds tipped over an irrigation pivot.  Blowing dust was also observed.  A nearby mesonet site measured the peak gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around midnight a northward moving thunderstorm produced wind gusts strong enough to blow power poles down in both Yuma and Washington Counties.  After the storm moved through, the unseasonably strong winds continued for a third day in a row over East Central Colorado.  What makes this more unusual is the high winds started after midnight, which does not even occur when winds of this magnitude are more common.  The peak high wind gust was in Cheyenne County of 83 MPH near Firstview.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred as outflow winds from behind the thunderstorm moved through.  The winds also caused blowing dust to occur, reducing the visibility to four miles at the Yuma airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms developed in eastern Colorado which moved eastward toward the CO/KS border in the afternoon hours. The thunderstorms produced wind gusts up to 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported a wind gust measured at 60 mph at location.

A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph was measured near Firstview, CO during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station recorded a non-thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph 3.7 miles east of Firstview, CO.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed off the Rockies in a line north of I-70 with a few clusters of storms to the south of I-70. As the storms crossed into Yuma County, they produced 60 mph wind gusts. The 60 mph wind gusts created blowing dust that reduced visibility in northern Yuma County causing a car to drive into a ditch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Emergency Manager reported a car in a ditch on State Highway 59 about 5 miles north of Yuma with zero visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in eastern Colorado organizing into a line as they neared the Kansas border with isolated storms forming to the south of Cheyenne County in Colorado. The line of storms produced thunderstorm wind gusts up to 65 mph and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS observed 58 mph gusts with 43 mph sustained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around midnight a northward moving thunderstorm produced wind gusts strong enough to blow power poles down in both Yuma and Washington Counties.  After the storm moved through, the unseasonably strong winds continued for a third day in a row over East Central Colorado.  What makes this more unusual is the high winds started after midnight, which does not even occur when winds of this magnitude are more common.  The peak high wind gust was in Cheyenne County of 83 MPH near Firstview.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unknown number of power poles near the intersection of Highway 59 and CR 54 were blown down.
